content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 270 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2PE_002_page_001.jpg

1. But there arose also false prophets among the people, just as there will be false teachers among you, who will secretly bring in destructive heresies, even denying the Master who bought them, bringing upon themselves swift destruction.

2. And many will follow their shameless ways, and because of them the way of truth will be blasphemed.

3. And in covetousness they will exploit you with fabricated words; for whom the judgment from of old is not idle, and their destruction does not slumber.

4. For if God did not spare the angels who sinned, but cast them into Tartarus and delivered them to chains of darkness, to be kept for judgment;

5. and did not spare the ancient world, but preserved Noah, the eighth person, a herald of righteousness, when he brought a flood upon the world of the ungodly;

6. and condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, having made them an example of what is coming upon the ungodly;

7. and rescued righteous Lot, who was distressed by the conduct of the lawless in their shameless ways --

8. for that righteous man, dwelling among them day after day, was tormented in his righteous soul by what he saw and heard of their lawless deeds --

9. the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial, and to keep the unrighteous under punishment for the day of judgment,

10. and especially those who go after the flesh in the lust of defilement and despise authority. Bold and self-willed, they do not tremble when they blaspheme the glorious ones,

11. whereas angels, though greater in strength and power, do not bring a blasphemous judgment against them before the Lord.

12. But these, like irrational animals, creatures of instinct, born for capture and destruction, blaspheming in matters of which they are ignorant, will in their destruction also be destroyed,

13. suffering wrong as the wages of wrongdoing. They count it a pleasure to revel in the daytime. They are stains and blemishes, reveling in their deceptions while they feast together with you.

14. Having eyes full of adultery and unceasing from sin, they entice unstable souls. They have a heart trained in covetousness -- accursed children!

15. Forsaking the straight way, they went astray, having followed the way of Balaam the son of Bosor, who loved the wages of unrighteousness,

16. but received a rebuke for his own transgression: a speechless donkey, speaking with a human voice, restrained the madness of the prophet.

17. These are waterless springs and mists driven by a storm, for whom the gloom of darkness has been reserved.

18. For speaking arrogant words of vanity, they entice by fleshly desires, by shameless deeds, those who are barely escaping from those who live in error,

19. promising them freedom while they themselves are slaves of corruption; for by whatever a person is overcome, by this he is enslaved.

20. For if, having escaped the defilements of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, they are again entangled and overcome by them, the last state has become worse for them than the first.

21. For it would have been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness than, having known it, to turn back from the holy commandment delivered to them.

22. It has happened to them according to the true proverb: "A dog returns to its own vomit," and, "A sow, having washed, returns to wallowing in the mire."

---

## Translator's Note

2 Peter 2 is the most sustained polemic against false teachers in the New Testament. The author builds a devastating case through three historical precedents -- the fallen angels (v.4), the flood generation (v.5), and the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah (v.6) -- demonstrating that God's judgment on the wicked is certain. Yet interwoven with each judgment is a corresponding rescue: Noah is preserved through the flood, Lot is extracted from Sodom. Verse 9 crystallizes the dual principle: the Lord knows how to rescue the godly and to keep the unrighteous for judgment.

The chapter's vocabulary is notably rare. "Tartarus" (v.4) is a hapax legomenon in the entire New Testament, borrowed from Greek mythology for the prison of fallen angels. "Reducing to ashes" (v.6, tephrosoas) likewise appears only here. The Sinaiticus reading "chains of darkness" (seirais zophou, v.4) is followed rather than the variant "pits" (sirois) found in later manuscripts. Similarly, Sinaiticus reads "deceptions" (apatais, v.13) rather than "love-feasts" (agapais), and "barely" (oligos, v.18) rather than "truly" (ontos).

The chapter closes with two proverbs of deliberate disgust -- the dog returning to vomit (Proverbs 26:11) and the washed sow returning to the mud (an extra-biblical saying). The point is not cruelty but clarity: the nature that was never truly changed will reassert itself. The false teachers' apostasy is not a stumble but a revelation of character.
